other_observers: David Trappett, Diane Trappett; I do not know who the original observer was.  Others who observed the bird: Larry Arnold 2011-11-19 Nic Korte 2011-11-20
description: We observed a small sized female humming bird. While perched the wingtips projected just beyond the tail length. Pale underparts were very visible and pale area above the eye was noted although harder to pick out.  A photo is attached.  In addition, this bird was captured earlier in the day by a hummingbird bander. Here is his description:  "We set up two hummingbird traps, one in place of the existing feeder and another in a new location. It wasn't long before the bird came to check out the new feeder and we gently closed the trap door. After banding, taking data and a few photos, the bird returned to the feeder immediately after being released. She weighed 3.2 grams which is substantial and healthy for a Costa's, but she had no visible fat reserves which suggests she may be done migrating for now. Given our experience with the wintering hummers in the past, I believe this hummingbird will do just fine so long as insects and sugar water are available." - Steve BoriciousBehaviors: The bird was sitting for long periods of time in bushes about 25 feet away from a feeder on a porch. It did visit the feeder for short amounts of time between sitting. The bird was totally silent while we watched.Call: No vocalizations while I was present.Plumage: adult
